title: Whakairo Awa: Carving the River's Story | Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku - He Whakairo, He Kōrero
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the art of whakairo (carving) through the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway to Ngāti Kahungunu. The awa has provided sustenance, transportation, and spiritual connection for generations. This lesson connects to local pūrākau (legends) about the taniwha protector of the awa and examines how contemporary carving practices can preserve these stories while addressing modern environmental challenges facing the waterway.
learningObjectives: Create a small whakairo piece that visually interprets one pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, demonstrating understanding of traditional Māori design elements (e.g., koru, pikorua)
Analyze and explain how at least three different carving materials (wood, stone, modern composite) affect the expression and durability of cultural stories

Collaboratively develop and present a proposal for a public art installation along the Mangakōtukutuku Stream that addresses an environmental issue (e.g., pollution, habitat loss) through cultural narrative

activities.title: Awa Kōrero | River Narratives
activities.description: Students visit the Mangakōtukutuku Stream with a local kaumātua who shares pūrākau (legends) of the awa, particularly those involving its taniwha protector. Students sketch natural forms and patterns observed at the riverbank and record key narrative elements. Back in class, they analyze how these natural patterns translate into traditional whakairo motifs.

activities.title: Mātāpono o te Whakairo | Principles of Carving
activities.description: A local carver from Ngāti Kahungunu demonstrates basic whakairo techniques using different materials (wood, pounamu replica stone, modern materials). Students practice these techniques on soap or soft wood, focusing on creating patterns inspired by their river sketches and the shared pūrākau.

activities.title: Taiao me te Toi | Environment and Art
activities.description: Students examine the current environmental state of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream through water testing and observation. They discuss how environmental degradation affects cultural preservation. In groups, they brainstorm how a whakairo installation could both tell the stream's stories and raise awareness about its environmental challenges.

activities.title: He Whakairo mō Āpōpō | Carving for Tomorrow
activities.description: Using their developed skills and understanding, students create a small individual whakairo piece that interprets one aspect of the awa's story. They must choose their material deliberately and be prepared to explain how their material choice affects the storytelling and durability of their work.

activities.title: Whakaaturanga Toi | Art Exhibition
activities.description: Students present their individual carvings and group installation proposals in a classroom gallery walk. They explain their creative choices, material selections, and how their work contributes to preserving the stories and protecting the future of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu to share pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Master carver from local marae for technique demonstration
The Māori Carver: Whakairo Rakau by Sidney Moko Mead (book)
Toi Te Rito Maihi: Contemporary Māori Carving (online resource from Auckland Art Gallery)

tasks: Creation of individual whakairo piece with artist statement
Written analysis of material choices and cultural significance
Group presentation of proposed public art installation

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: Investigate the characteristics, uses, and interpretations of whakairo in past and present cultural contexts
Developing Practical Knowledge: Explore and use tools, materials, processes, and conventions of whakairo
Developing Ideas: Generate, develop, and refine sculpture ideas in response to a local cultural and environmental context
Communicating and Interpreting: Present and respond to artwork, describing how whakairo expresses and records community values and stories
